# The ASI Stack

**The ASI Stack: A Systems Architecture for Governed, Efficient, Self-Improving AI** is a living technical book by Corben Sorenson.

## Current status

::: {.living-dashboard}
<div>
<strong>Book state</strong>
<span>Scaffold ready for drafting</span>
</div>
<div>
<strong>Sources</strong>
<span>Inventoried; cache status tracked</span>
</div>
<div>
<strong>Claims</strong>
<span>Argument-level until evidence moves</span>
</div>
<div>
<strong>Tests</strong>
<span>Planned; not yet run</span>
</div>
:::

## Core thesis

Efficient ASI should not be treated as one giant opaque model. It should be treated as a governed cognitive stack: alignment, governance, planning, memory, reasoning, execution, routing, compression, evidence, and recursive self-improvement cooperating through typed boundaries, verification, artifact memory, and bounded authority.

## Architecture at a glance

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
  A[Human intent] --> B[Alignment and constitution]
  B --> C[Governance and authority]
  C --> D[Planning and control]
  D --> E[Virtual context memory]
  E --> F[Routing and modular intelligence]
  F --> G[Reasoning and verification]
  G --> H[Labor and execution OS]
  H --> I[Artifacts and runtime outputs]
  I --> J[Evidence and benchmark ratchets]
  J --> K[Governed self-improvement]
  K --> C
```

## How to read

Start with Part I for the architecture thesis and failure model. Then read Part II as the layer-by-layer specification. Part III explains how the layers integrate, how to prototype them, and how the book itself stays alive.

The appendices are part of the architecture:

- Appendix A tracks source assignments.
- Appendix C tracks claim support states.
- Appendix D tracks protocol schemas.
- Appendix E tracks planned Codex tests.
- Appendix F tracks living updates.

## Evidence discipline

Every major claim should carry a support state. The default state is `argument`. Claims move only when sources are actually read, prototypes are inspected, or tests are actually run. This book should never pretend that source ingestion or test evidence exists before it does.
